# Soap Duck — Technical Documentation

> A bubble-bath themed endless-runner in the spirit of the Chrome dino game. You
> play a rubber duck dodging shampoo bottles and sponges, collecting soap, and
> spending it on a persistent skill tree between runs. Pure TypeScript + HTML5
> Canvas, no game engine, no runtime dependencies.

*This document was generated by exploring the project's knowledge graph
(`graphify-out/`): 169 nodes, 406 edges, 10 communities. It is organized around
the communities the graph surfaced, anchored on the highest-centrality "god
nodes" (`updateGame()`, `GameState`, `drawScene()`, `buildRunConfig()`, `step()`).*

---

## 1. At a glance

| | |
|---|---|
| **Package** | `soap-dino` v0.1.0 (private) |
| **Language** | TypeScript 5.4 (ES modules, `strict`) |
| **Build/dev** | Vite 5 |
| **Tests** | Vitest 1.6 (node environment, 16 test files) |
| **Runtime deps** | None — only the browser Canvas API |
| **Render target** | A single `<canvas id="game" width="800" height="300">` |
| **Source size** | ~1,750 lines of TS across 35 files |

### Commands

```bash
npm run dev        # vite dev server with HMR
npm run build      # tsc typecheck + vite production build
npm run preview    # serve the production build
npm test           # vitest run (one-shot)
npm run test:watch # vitest in watch mode
```

There is no install step beyond `npm install` (dev dependencies only). Open the
dev server and the game runs immediately — `index.html` loads `src/main.ts` as a
module against the `#game` canvas.

---

## 2. Architecture overview

The codebase is a clean **functional core / imperative shell**. Almost every
module is a set of pure functions operating on plain state objects; the only
stateful, side-effecting code lives in `src/main.ts` (the shell) and the
`startLoop` driver. This is why the test suite can cover game logic without a
DOM — the simulation never touches the canvas.

Data flows in one direction each frame:

```
            ┌──────────────────────────── src/main.ts (the shell) ──────────────────────────┐
            │                                                                                │
  keyboard ─┤  createInput ──► Input state                                                   │
            │                       │                                                        │
            │                       ▼                                                        │
  startLoop ┤   step(dt) ──► updateGame(GameState, Input, dt) ──► mutates GameState          │
 (fixed dt) │                       │            │                                           │
            │                       │            ├─ updatePlayer / jump / fastFall (physics) │
            │                       │            ├─ updateSpawner (obstacles + soap)         │
            │                       │            ├─ intersects (AABB collision)              │
            │                       │            └─ soapValue (run economy)                  │
            │                       ▼                                                        │
            │   render(alpha) ──► drawScene / drawHud / drawMenu / drawSkillTree ──► canvas  │
            │                                                                                │
            │   on game over: bankRun(save,…) ──► writeSave  (localStorage)                  │
            │   in skill tree: purchase(id,save) ──► writeSave                               │
            └────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

### The central bridge: `updateGame()`

The graph flags `updateGame()` as the single most connected node (16 edges,
highest betweenness centrality at 0.035). That is structurally accurate: it is
the seam where every subsystem meets. In one function call it reads **input**,
drives **player physics**, ticks the **spawner**, runs **collision**, and applies
the run **economy** — then `main.ts` takes its results and routes them to
**rendering** and **save storage**. Understanding `updateGame()`
(`src/game/Game.ts:73`) is understanding the whole game.

### The configuration spine: `RunConfig`

The second structural truth the graph reveals is `RunConfig`
(`src/skills/effects.ts:3`). The skill tree never reaches into gameplay code
directly. Instead, owned skills are compiled once per run into a flat `RunConfig`
struct by `buildRunConfig()`, and gameplay reads only that struct. This keeps a
hard boundary between *progression* (skills, save data) and *simulation*
(physics, spawning), and is why those live in separate graph communities that
touch only through `RunConfig`.

---

## 3. Module reference (by graph community)

The graph clustered the code into 10 communities. The seven meaningful ones map
cleanly onto subsystems; the remaining three are config islands.

### 3.1 Core Gameplay & Physics — `src/game/`, `src/engine/input.ts`, `src/config.ts`

The simulation core. 42 nodes — the largest community.

| File | Responsibility |
|------|----------------|
| `game/Game.ts` | `GameState` shape, `createGame()`, and `updateGame()` — the per-tick simulation. |
| `game/entities/Player.ts` | Duck physics: `jump`, `setDuck`, `fastFall`, `updatePlayer`, and the `playerAABB` hitbox. |
| `game/spawner.ts` | Time-based obstacle/soap spawning and the speed ramp (`currentSpeed`). |
| `game/collision.ts` | `AABB` type + `intersects()` — the only collision primitive. |
| `game/economy.ts` | `soapValue()` (per-soap worth) and `bankRun()` (commit run results to save). |
| `engine/input.ts` | `createInput()` — keyboard listeners producing an `Input` state with edge-triggered presses. |
| `config.ts` | Tunable constants (canvas size, gravity, speeds) and the `ObstacleType` union. |

**Player physics** (`Player.ts`). `y` is height above the ground (0 = grounded),
`vy` positive = upward. Gravity pulls `vy` down each tick; landing resets
`jumpsUsed` to 0, which is what makes `maxJumps` (double-jump) work. The hitbox
(`playerAABB`) shrinks when ducking and shrinks further with the `hitboxShrink`
skill — collision is computed against this box, not the sprite.

**Input** (`input.ts`) uses an *edge vs. held* distinction. `jumpPressed`,
`downPressed`, `restart`, and the ability keys are one-shot edges cleared every
frame by `clearEdges()` (called from `main.ts`); `duckHeld` is a level that stays
true while ArrowDown is down. This prevents auto-repeat from firing a jump every
frame.

**Spawning** (`spawner.ts`). The spawner holds `nextObstacleAt` / `nextSoapAt`
timestamps. Obstacle gaps shrink as the world speeds up (`baseGap / speedFactor`)
with ±30% jitter; obstacle type is weighted 45% small shampoo / 35% large
shampoo / 20% sponge (`pickObstacle`). All randomness is injected via an `rng`
parameter (defaulting to `Math.random`) so tests are deterministic.

**`updateGame()` tick order** (matters for correctness):
1. Apply input to player (jump / duck / fast-fall).
2. Trigger abilities (shield arm, slow-mo, screen-clear) respecting cooldowns.
3. Tick cooldowns and the slow-mo timer; derive `worldScale` (0.5 during slow-mo).
4. `updatePlayer` (gravity integration).
5. Advance world time, compute speed and `move`, accumulate distance and score.
6. `updateSpawner` → push new obstacles/soap.
7. Move entities left; apply the soap-magnet drift if unlocked.
8. Soap collection (AABB), add `soapValue` to `runSoap`.
9. Obstacle collision → shield absorbs, else spend a life, else `over = true`.

### 3.2 Game Loop & Bootstrap — `src/main.ts`, `src/engine/loop.ts`, `src/render/background.ts`, `src/render/effects.ts`

How the game starts and keeps ticking. Contains the god node `step()`.

**Fixed-timestep loop** (`engine/loop.ts`). `startLoop` runs a classic
accumulator: real frame time feeds `stepAccumulator`, which emits a whole number
of fixed 1/60 s steps (capped at `MAX_STEPS = 5` to avoid the "spiral of death"
after a stall) plus a fractional `alpha` passed to render. `onStep` always
receives `dt = 1/60`, so the simulation is frame-rate independent and
deterministic. `stepAccumulator` is a pure function and is unit-tested in
isolation.

**The shell** (`main.ts`). Owns all mutable runtime state: the `Screen` state
machine (`menu | playing | gameover | tree`), the current `GameState | null`,
the loaded `save`, the bubble field, and the particle effect list. It wires DOM
events (keydown for screen transitions, mouse for skill-tree interaction) and
contains the only two `step`/`render` functions handed to `startLoop`. Screen
transitions: SPACE starts a run from menu/tree; death → `gameover` (1.2 s) →
`tree`; T opens the tree from the menu.

**Ambient visuals** (`background.ts`, `effects.ts`) live here because they are
driven directly by the loop, not by gameplay. `background.ts` is the rising
bubble field + scrolling foam line (a `BubbleField` of drifting circles).
`effects.ts` is a lightweight particle system: `spawnBurst` emits a `pop` (rising
white bubbles when soap is collected) or `splash` (cyan droplets on death).

### 3.3 Sprite Rendering & Scenes — `src/render/scene.ts`, `src/render/sprites.ts`, `src/ui/menu.ts`

Turning `GameState` into pixels. Contains the god node `drawScene()`.

- **`sprites.ts`** is the pixel-art engine. `PixelGrid` is a `number[][]`; the
  `P()` helper builds grids from ASCII rows (`'#'` → filled). `drawPixels`
  paints a grid at a scale using a palette array (index 0 = transparent). All the
  duck/shampoo/sponge/soap sprite maps are defined here, plus `roundRectPath`
  (shared rounded-rectangle helper used by HUD and skill tree).
- **`scene.ts`** (`drawScene`) composes a frame: background → obstacles → soaps →
  player → effects. It selects the duck sprite by state (jumping / ducking /
  two-frame run cycle) and maps each `ObstacleType` to its sprite+palette
  (`obstacleSprite`). This is a pure read of `GameState`; it never mutates.
- **`menu.ts`** (`drawMenu`) is the title screen — decorative duck, "SOAP DUCK",
  high score, and controls.

### 3.4 Skill Tree — `src/skills/tree.ts`, `src/ui/skillTree.ts`

Persistent progression. The model/view split is strict.

- **`skills/tree.ts`** (model) defines `SKILL_TREE`: 19 nodes across four
  branches — `movement`, `economy`, `active`, `passive` — each with a `cost`,
  `prereqs`, and description. `canPurchase` enforces affordability + prerequisites
  + not-already-owned; `purchase` returns a *new* `SaveData` (immutable update)
  with soap deducted and the node added.
- **`ui/skillTree.ts`** (view) lays the four branches out in columns
  (`layoutTree`), hit-tests mouse clicks (`nodeAtPoint`), and renders node boxes
  with owned/affordable/locked styling (`drawSkillTree`).

The tree is consumed by gameplay only indirectly — see `RunConfig` below.

### 3.5 Economy & Save Storage — `src/game/economy.ts`, `src/save/storage.ts`

The persistence boundary. `SaveData` = `{ totalSoap, unlockedNodes[], highScore }`.

- **`storage.ts`** abstracts persistence behind a `StorageLike` interface
  (`getItem`/`setItem`), defaulting to `localStorage` under key
  `soap_dino_save`. `loadSave` defensively validates every field and falls back
  to `defaultSave()` on missing/corrupt data, so a bad save can never crash the
  game. Injecting a fake `StorageLike` is what makes save logic testable.
- **`economy.ts`** bridges a finished run into persistent state: `bankRun` adds
  `runSoap` to `totalSoap` and raises `highScore`. `soapValue` reads the run's
  `soapMultiplier`.

> **Graph note:** `economy.ts` and `storage.ts` cluster together (community 5) and
> are reachable from gameplay only through `updateGame()` → `bankRun`/`writeSave`
> in the shell. This is the "Core Gameplay → Economy & Save Storage" bridge the
> graph highlights on `updateGame()`.

### 3.6 HUD & Color Palettes — `src/render/hud.ts`, `src/render/palette.ts`

- **`palette.ts`** is the single source of color truth — bubble-bath theme
  colors plus four indexed sprite palettes (duck, soap, shampoo, sponge). Index 0
  is the transparent slot `drawPixels` skips. Nearly every render module imports
  from here, which is why it has high fan-in despite being a tiny constants file.
- **`hud.ts`** (`drawHud`) draws the in-run overlay: score, soap counter, and the
  Z/X/C ability bubbles dimmed while on cooldown.

### 3.7 Config islands — `package.json`, `tsconfig.json`, `vite.config.ts`, `sanity.test.ts`

The graph isolated each of these as its own tiny community because they share no
symbols with the rest of the code. `tsconfig.json` runs `strict` with
`noUnusedLocals`/`noUnusedParameters` and `noEmit` (Vite handles emit;
`tsc` is typecheck-only in `build`). `vite.config.ts` is actually a *Vitest*
config (node environment, global test APIs). `sanity.test.ts` is a standalone
smoke test.

---

## 4. The skill system in depth (cross-cutting)

This is the most interesting flow in the codebase because it threads through four
communities while keeping them decoupled. The decoupling agent is `RunConfig`.

```
SaveData.unlockedNodes  ──buildRunConfig()──►  RunConfig  ──►  read by gameplay
   (skills/tree model)     (skills/effects.ts)                 (Game, Player, spawner, economy)
```

`buildRunConfig(unlockedNodes)` (`src/skills/effects.ts:18`) starts from a
baseline config and toggles/accumulates fields per owned node:

| Skill id(s) | Effect on `RunConfig` | Where it's read |
|-------------|----------------------|-----------------|
| `doubleJump` | `maxJumps = 2` | `Player.jump` |
| `higherJump` | `jumpVelocity ×1.18` | `Player.jump` |
| `fastFall` | `canFastFall = true` | `Player.fastFall` |
| `duckSlide` | `canDuckSlide = true` | `Player.playerAABB` (shorter hitbox) |
| `slowRamp` | `speedRampMultiplier = 0.6` | `spawner.currentSpeed` |
| `extraLife` | `extraLives = 1` | `Game.createGame` → `livesLeft` |
| `soapMagnet` | `soapMagnet = true` | `updateGame` soap drift |
| `soapX2` | `soapMultiplier = 2` | `economy.soapValue` |
| `abilityShield/Slowmo/Clear` | `abilities.* = true` | `updateGame` ability handling |
| `score1/2/3` | `scoreMultiplier += 0.25` each | `updateGame` scoring |
| `drop1/2/3` | `soapDropRate += 0.5` each | `spawner.updateSpawner` |
| `hitbox1/2` | `hitboxShrink += 4` each | `Player.playerAABB` |

The config is built **once** in `startRun()` (`main.ts:36`) and frozen for the
duration of the run. Buying a skill mid-game has no effect until the next run —
which is the intended roguelite loop: run → earn soap → spend on the tree → run
stronger.

---

## 5. Testing

16 `*.test.ts` files sit beside their modules, run under Vitest in a **node**
environment (no DOM). The functional-core design is what makes this possible:
every piece of game logic is a pure function over plain objects, and all
randomness and storage are dependency-injected.

- Determinism via injected `rng: () => number` in `spawner`, `effects`,
  `background`, and `updateGame`.
- Persistence via injected `StorageLike` in `storage` (tests pass a fake store).
- The render and DOM-bound modules (`scene`, `hud`, `menu`, `skillTree`,
  `main`, `loop`'s `requestAnimationFrame` path) are intentionally thin wrappers;
  the testable logic (`stepAccumulator`, `layoutTree`, `nodeAtPoint`, sprite
  helpers, palettes) is split out into pure functions and covered directly.

Run `npm test` for a one-shot pass or `npm run test:watch` while developing.

---

## 6. Extending the game

A few common changes, with the minimal set of files to touch:

- **Add an obstacle type:** extend the `ObstacleType` union in `config.ts`, add a
  case to `obstacleBox` (`Game.ts`) and `pickObstacle` (`spawner.ts`), and map a
  sprite/palette in `obstacleSprite` (`scene.ts`). Add the sprite grid in
  `sprites.ts`.
- **Add a skill:** append a `SkillNode` to `SKILL_TREE` (`skills/tree.ts`) and
  handle its id in `buildRunConfig` (`skills/effects.ts`). The tree UI lays it out
  automatically from its `branch`.
- **Retune difficulty:** edit constants in `config.ts` (`GRAVITY`, `BASE_SPEED`,
  `SPEED_RAMP`, `MAX_SPEED`, jump velocities) — they are all centralized.
- **Reskin:** edit `palette.ts` (colors) and the sprite grids in `sprites.ts`.

Because gameplay only ever reads `RunConfig` and the `config.ts` constants, new
mechanics almost always reduce to "add a field to `RunConfig`, set it in
`buildRunConfig`, read it in the one place it matters."

---

*Graph artifacts: `graphify-out/graph.html` (interactive), `graphify-out/GRAPH_REPORT.md`
(audit), `graphify-out/graph.json` (raw). Regenerate with `/graphify .`.*
